data.title: Firefly Aerospace Stock Surges, But I Still See A Speculative Buy
data.description:
Firefly Aerospace (FLY) stock surged 27% following several announcements over the past few days that the market seemingly is conceiving as positives. While the stock price has surged, we note that the price is still down 46% since my last report, when I marked the stock a speculative buy. I initiated coverage with a sell rating in August, and perhaps I was too quick to turn bullish on the name. In this report, I discuss the announcements and update my price target for Firefly Aerospace.

A Flurry Of Positive News For Firefly Aerospace

If we look at the share price development, we see that the stock price started increasing in November. This was driven by earnings that did miss on EPS, but beat on revenues with a positive sales outlook and also triggered a positive view from Deutsche Bank. I believe that momentum would have carried on if it weren’t for a shareholder selling to dispose of shares. Subsequently, the stock price fell to $16.00, which was around 15% lower than where I believe we could reasonably find support based on the expected results for 2025. In hindsight, that would provide a relatively shielded entry point for investors. From December onward, the stock price gained substantially. On the ninth of December, the news came that the company was added to the Russell 2000 Index and the Russell 3000 effective December 22nd. That provides a lot more exposure for Firefly Aerospace. On December 10th, the company announced an agreement with Volta Space Technologies to host a wireless power receiver on the second Blue Ghost Mission to the far side of the moon. That agreement does show that Firefly Aerospace is a trusted partner to host technology demonstrations. It may not necessarily be meaningful to revenues, but it does signal a lot of trust in Firefly’s launch capabilities.

While writing this report, another development was announced, and that is the appointment of Ramon Sanchez as COO of Firefly Aerospace. Sanchez brings two decades of experience and comes from Boeing and will be tasked with production scaling and operational execution.

Generally, the announcements this month have been positive. However, at the same time, we note that neither of the announcements brings a substantial justification for higher share prices in the near term. The share price popped, driven by improved sentiment on higher exposure due to the Russell Index inclusion, executive appointment, and a commercial technology demonstration agreement.

Revisiting The Firefly Aerospace Valuation

If we look at the expectations for cash, debt, and sales and apply an EV/Sales multiple of three times the aerospace and defense EV/Sales, which I believe is decent for emerging space companies given that Rocket Lab’s EV/Sales median is almost four times the peer group multiple, we get to the price targets as projected in the table. We see that the price to which Firefly Aerospace fell earlier this year is not completely arbitrary. It is very close to the price level that is supported by the EV/Sales valuation for 2025. If we look ahead at 2026, we would get around 60% upside with a $37.38 price target. I did lower my price target from $47.13 to $37.38 driven by a lower EV/Sales and a lower net cash position partially offset by a higher expected sales performance for 2026. The price target aligns reasonably well with the $37 price target from Wall Street analysts.

The Risks And Opportunities For Firefly Aerospace

Firefly Aerospace has been the first commercial party with a successful Moon landing. That does attract a lot of interest from potential partners in space and investors. However, we should keep in mind that with companies that are in their early stages of deploying engineering solutions in the commercial space, there is always the risk of mission failures or setbacks in development. One example is the ground test failure of the first stage of the Alpha rocket in September. Setbacks like these are part of the engineering testing and development of rockets, but they can ultimately result in setbacks on timelines and cash requirements. At all times, investors should be aware of similar risks as well as intensifying competition among emerging space companies.

Conclusion: Firefly Aerospace Stock Remains Attractive

For Firefly Aerospace, I maintain a positive view. I have lowered my price target but believe that the stock remains attractive for a speculative buy position. The major opportunity for Firefly Aerospace is the commercialization of space, with Firefly Aerospace being positioned as one of the few companies with a vertically integrated product offering once all products become fully developed and certified.

data.title: Vision Investing With Cirrus Logic
data.description:
Perhaps investors have developed multiple approaches to generating successful monetary returns. In our view, there are two, one being vision, understanding and relying on an accurately researched vision of future potential. This approach generally ignores short-term financial model reports. Reports such as these offer little long-term insight. Models, with significantly less value, might include short-term or long-term growth histories, valuation, discounted cash flows (DCF), earnings or other financial analyses. Instead of monitoring financials, the prudent investor research's market potential, financial and/or product barriers (competitors), capital security (does it have access to enough cash to survive the early years), synergy, compelling products, product development timing, and probably the most important, human capital or better stated, development execution. Relating to the last, both product development and production become paramount. In our view, vision investments must have both development and production excellence because without one, the approaching financial model collapses.

In this article, our choice of example is Cirrus Logic, Inc. (CRUS), an investment we have owned for more than three decades. This continues our coverage of the company, the last being Cirrus Logic's Moderately Valued Price Suggests Patience. In the article, we argued for patience due to our belief that lower stock prices were likely. Prices in the low to mid-hundred teens did come and go. We would have bought shares in that middle range.

Yet, it appears that the eye-doc's tools stated above are helpful in spotting long-term vision investments like Cirrus Logic. Let's go find out how good!

Long-term Vision: A Valuable Investment Style

We religiously followed, and still do, Peter Lynch's principle-based advice: invest in what you know, look for contrarian value and don't prune the buds. Of particular note, this is primarily principle and potential-based rather than heavily weighted in finances. His success was unprecedented.

A Vision Reviewed

Our Cirrus interest came from an article appearing late in a 1994 Money Magazine discussing the extremely low valuation of the stock less than a tenth of its recent high, Peter Lynch's second principle. The company in 95 primarily produced state-of-the-art graphic accelerators for PCs. Yet, a few years earlier, Cirrus purchased an Austin-based start-up, Crystal Logic, with its patent-protected state-of-the-art mixed-signal products, chips with both an analog and digital component. The role of this technology provides an interface between the analog and digital worlds. This is an extremely difficult endeavor, with digital producing troublesome noise cascading into the analog function. Most applications, in particular sound or voice can't tolerate noisy analog processes, which is Crystal's targeted market. Our interest in the up-and-down progress with Cirrus became its mixed-signal potential, a mirror of our profession. This illustrates our adherence to Lynch's first principle: invest in what you know. We dealt with this daily at work. The extreme difficulty in developing this technology provides a natural entrance barrier. Today, Cirrus is almost completely alone having purchased the only real audio competitor, England-based Wolfson, several years ago.

Although this sound market remained small through the 90s, a truly transformative period opened early in the second millennium, when Steve Jobs returned to Apple Inc. (AAPL). His vision of targeting mobile markets with transformative products, first the iPod followed by iPhone, iPad, iWatch and others, opened huge doors for Cirrus' technology. In 2007, the company won, from Wolfson, the codec with its ability to internally integrate a capacitance function. Cirrus shortly afterward developed and won the amplifier market. With mobile, optimizing three critical attributes became paramount: size, power usage (lowest being best) and noiseless performance. Cirrus developed the best-in-class optimization. This remains so.

Continuing to discuss the perfect match, Apple's planned demand perfectly matched execution to coincide with its plans. A new management team set out to create the then almost non-existent human capital for both product and manufacturing. Cirrus successfully created a best-in-class employee work environment in the early period. We watched intently and closely for it was with this that the immense market size coming with Apple would materialize. This remains a critical attribute.

We noted above the critical value of understanding, valuing, and trusting in market potential. How is it sized? How long will the market last? Can the products be improved, or will natural attrition eventually replace them? With Apple Computer's entrance into the mobile markets and shortening its name in 2007 to Apple, enormous potential approached though exact amounts remained fuzzy.

In addition, product life plus product development cycles add critical investment timing for holding and buying shares of stock. Product development cycles may create an illusion of no growth with massive growth lurking in the near future. Product longevity or lack thereof significantly impacts investment strategies.

Value in Synergy

One additional mostly undervalued element, synergy, adds depth and security to the investment. In the early days, Cirrus had a high-margin product market with A/D D/A interfacing for industrial, power monitoring, seismic testing, professional and others. The technology developed for this market in essence paid the bills for developing audio and sound. With audio more secure in place, the company is now developing the next extension, power functions, which are still undetermined for both sides of the battery. We will discuss more details later. Management also extended the reach of the audio into laptop markets. The strange entrance was a direct result of competitors in this market no longer being able to provide parts adequately during 2020 and 2021. The customers soon experienced the Cirrus product superiority and are now designing with custom PC parts.

Defining Vision vs. Execution Investments

We noted two types, the second is execution based, the transition between the two is primarily defined in our view, which is the dog and which is the tail. It is about potential. Although definitions might be personal, this difference matters. Knowing which parameters remain paramount helps investors decide on an investment approach, buy/sell, then reload or sell short calls for periods of time that present themselves as enriching opportunities. Understanding this difference also helps investors pay attention to proper metrics. Finances mostly take a second seat, if any seat at all. It is mostly about potential and faith that this potential is at least somewhat real.

With companies that have reached a level of execution, obviously, paying attention to established business performance is still very important. For Cirrus, the last quarterly report beat significantly analysts' expectations. From a Seeking Alpha headline, Cirrus Logic Non-GAAP EPS of $2.83 beats by $0.45, revenue of $561M beats by $19.59M, bullish reports continued illustrating the strong health of the business performance for the execution portion. For the year, the business is on target for between $8 - $8.5, creating a reasonable value for stock prices in the $110 - $130 range.

Still A Vision Investment

In our view, Cirrus remains a vision investment with lots of somewhat fuzzy growth opportunities coming, even strong possibilities for revenue to more than double. Before we discuss in more detail future growth, John Forsyth, CEO, during the last call, reminded investors of this:

We consider this longevity a significant strength of our business as it provides solid long-term visibility and sustained revenue contribution, along with the ability to leverage our R&D resources in new areas that can drive further innovation and growth.

Forsyth just told us that new business profits fall directly to the bottom line, a change from the past that has massive implications for earnings growth.

So, it's time to review the future with a level of added detail. Cirrus' future growth falls within three categories:

  • Control products on both sides of a battery.
  • PC audio and haptics.
  • High margin products such as industrial, pro-audio, auto, etc.

Beginning with the first, management now focuses resources, some once dedicated to new codec products, on the battery. A slide from the call could help investors more accurately understand the long-term path.

The graphic shows the several-year path of winning sockets primarily with Apple. Not in the slide, Cirrus lost a haptic socket worth approximately $1 a few years back because the companion part from another vendor failed. At that event, management noted its disappointment but included commentary that this loss over the long term was almost immaterial. The comment offers a window into the size of coming ASPs.

Answering a question from Tore Svanberg, of Stifel, on the battery-targeted products, Forsyth added,

And I think we've seen some validation of that from the comments reflected back to us from customers when we've been sharing silicon with them and details on what we've been developing. We've got a number of irons in the fire there.

The CEO also made clear that final products or design wins aren't yet.

In our view, the battery products include at least:

  • Power distribution.
    • ASPs $1 - $2 per phone.
    • Based on Lion Semiconductor technology (Capacitor).
    • Extremely efficient with simpler and smaller sizes.
  • Charging conversion.
    • Again, $1 - $2 ASP.
    • Based on Lion Semiconductor technology.

Although this product projection makes sense and is our best guess, we could be wrong. But, with the statement made by management concerning the $1 product, it seems to support the content ASP gains in the range predicted above.

Also, it seems important to note the importance of the recent fab partnership with Global Foundries, targeting U.S. fab locations with products of a very different structure. What, if any, role will this new technology add?

Should Cirrus win iPhone sockets, the major target, a $3 ASP increase adds $6 per share of profit at 250 million per year iPhone units sold. This is an approximate $100 change in stock value. These state-of-the-art products could easily displace older less efficient competitive technology within mid-to-flagship models outside of Apple. This would approximately double the potential market. A win with Apple could drive the stock price steeply higher.

Next, the PC audio, haptic and charging products are discussed. Again, a slide can help.

Cirrus Logic Presentation

The targeted SAM continues to increase, now in the $1.2B range.

A progress summary follows:

  • Both "consumer and commercial laptops featuring our components to come to market over the next year as the adoption of SoundWire device class audio accelerates," expands our footprint.
  • Cirrus released a new codec that delivers enhanced voice activation even in low-power modes.
  • Won the first mainstream-priced laptop.

The new features are part of the expanding SAM. With continued measured penetration, stock price effects are likely measured rather than steep.

The last class of new market targets comes in majorly upgraded general market products. Regarding these, management offered:

  • Progress toward gaining design wins significantly improved in the last few quarters.
  • Margins are considerably higher than corporate averages.
  • Long life cycles.
  • Increased engagement with automotive and professional audio customers with new timing products.
  • Sampling high-end analog front-end components.
  • $1B or more SAM.

Higher stock prices are likely measured generally following slower changes.

The summary of visionary growth describes future earnings potential. For example, each billion in new revenue adds $1B times 0.52 (margin) times 0.82 (taxes) divided by 50 million shares equaling approximately $10 per share. This compares to approximately $8 per share for the first $2B in revenue. Cirrus opportunistically targets at least an additional $3.5B in revenue of which it could win a very large percentage as past history suggests at least 50% of the targeted SAM. This is clearly about vision with demonstrated requisite execution.

Risks

Cirrus carries the Apple risk being by far the largest customer generally near 90%. In our view, Cirrus isn't risking losing sockets; it's about Apple performance going forward. On that issue, "Citi analysts expect iPhone sales to rise this year, driven by customers ready to upgrade older devices. The iPhone 17 has proved especially popular, with sales fueling a record year." Both Counterpoint and IDC have been confirming strong sales fueled mainly by a strong iPhone 17 product and a vast group of iPhone users in need of major upgrades. Also, the jury is still out on how far Cirrus can penetrate the PC world. We noticed an interesting misguided comment from Wedbush.

In the semiconductor and PC space, Wedbush sees the “surging computer memory demand” as squeezing companies tied to the traditional PC and handset spaces, negatively impacting Intel Corporation (INTC), HP Inc. (HPQ), Synaptics Incorporated (SYNA), QUALCOMM Incorporated (QCOM), Qorvo, Inc. (QRVO) and Cirrus Logic.

Really, this makes little sense being that at least for now the PC business is ultra small and going forward markets do change and whatever is there will be additive.

In our view, the biggest risk is for Cirrus to become a story stock, one that trades outside of any rational realm of financial fundamentals. We are selling short calls, thus we stand to lose all the stock should it become so.

Summary & Rating

Our stock rating for a buy came and went during the period in which we wrote this article; thus, we rate Cirrus a hold for now. We expected prices in the mid-$110 - $120. The prices came and went. It may come again.

From a summary standpoint, keys involved with vision investing reside in the following critical parameters.

  • Potential (It's about research).
  • Cash solvency.
  • Human talent.
  • Management integrity.

What isn't important and can be extremely misleading is financial metrics such as DCF or recent revenue growth, even a lack thereof for a few years. Rather, understand what's coming and the timing thereof. Cirrus carries three plus years in product cycle development and implementation. Two-year growth models can be misleading. Investors might wake up one morning and find significant growth seemingly from nowhere. We expect this with Cirrus, by the way.

The growth potential targeted at the bottom line is immense, driven by three very real vectors. Cirrus' past performance suggests that growth could triple earnings in the next three to four years. Of note, we wrote in one of our early articles that a price of $300 plus was possible. Although our timing suggested was way off, the reality of a significantly higher price for the company is more than possible. It is becoming more likely. As a reminder, we are long-term investors. We view investments by decades, not years. Should the price fall back into the $110 - $120 range, we would change the rating to buy once again. But, in our view, Cirrus will most likely significantly outperform the darlings of today going forward. Having help from the eye doctor's tools can be of massive help for investors.

data.title: Investing In The AI Era: Growth, Gains, And Risks
data.description:
By Magdalena Ocampo, Market Strategist

AI investment has not only dominated the equity landscape, but it has also become one of the few forces actively supporting the U.S. economy. In the first half of 2025, technology investment accounted for nearly half of the annual GDP growth, offsetting mounting pressures from trade uncertainty and shifting policy.

While consumer spending remains the primary driver of growth, AI's rapidly expanding role carries significant implications for investors. AI-driven productivity and economic gains could amplify the U.S. stock market rally, extending gains from a handful of dominant AI enablers to a broader group of AI adopters. But that momentum cuts both ways: any pullback in AI investment from major tech firms, or earnings that fail to justify the surge in spending, could quickly heighten economic risks.

After a year fueled by enthusiasm and accelerating innovation, markets may soon shift their attention from AI-powered optimism to the more challenging question of whether growth can be sustained into 2026.

From market phenomenon to macroeconomic force

Despite a cooling economy, the U.S. has shown resilience against various policy shocks this year, and current GDP forecasts predict trend growth in the U.S. economy through 2026 despite ongoing challenges, which has helped underpin equity markets.

Since the Federal Reserve began tightening in spring 2022, the S&P 500 has posted strong returns, buoyed by solid earnings and multiple expansion. The lion’s share of the S&P 500's strength, however, is largely attributed to the “Magnificent 7” tech giants, which have consistently reported strong earnings despite the broader economy facing headwinds. Their performance has provided a safety net for the broader index during times of volatility.

These companies, already leaders before the AI boom, have intensified their investments in AI infrastructure—including data centers, hardware, software, and large model development—to maintain their edge in the AI-driven technology revolution. This investment surge is expected to create new revenue opportunities and enhance profitability.

In essence, what began as a notable equity trend is evolving into a broader economic narrative. With projections indicating that AI-related capital expenditures will reach approximately $1.3 trillion over the next five years, AI is poised to translate capital investments into increased productivity and economic growth across the entire economy.

Divergence in earnings between the Magnificent 7 and the rest of the S&P 500’s constituents

EPS NTM, % appreciation last 3 years

AI’s contribution to economic growth

Though the government does not track AI investments separately, its significant impact on the economy is clear. From data center construction to spending on software and hardware, the recent surge in investment has bolstered the economy amid macroeconomic uncertainties.

AI capital expenditures contributed nearly one percentage point—almost half— to the 2.1% year-on-year growth in GDP during the first half of 2025. This suggests that without the recent wave of tech spending, the economy could have faced a more imminent recession. Even the slowdown in private investment, linked to tariff uncertainties, failed to restrain economic momentum, thanks in large part to AI-related tech investment. More importantly, AI-related investment is not limited to technology firms but has been adopted by companies across a myriad of sectors and industries, thereby acting as a growth driver for the economy as a whole.

It’s worth noting that a large share of the technology fueling this investment—particularly semiconductors and computing hardware—originates abroad, especially from Taiwan and China. This means that the direct contribution of tech spending to measured GDP growth is somewhat diluted once imports are taken into account. But this nuance doesn’t diminish AI’s broader economic influence. When incorporating the multiplier effects of AI—rising equity-market wealth, productivity gains as firms integrate AI into operations, and job creation tied to expanding AI infrastructure—the overall economic impact is likely far greater than what headline GDP data alone suggests.

Tech capex contribution to U.S. real GDP growth

Contributions to y/y % change in real GDP, Q1’23–Q2’25

AI as a buffer, not a replacement

Tech investment has strengthened the economy’s resilience. Still, uncertainty around the ultimate return on AI spending has shifted market attention toward whether growth can be sustained and whether AI will deliver the outcomes investors expect. Against that backdrop, the consumer remains the economy’s most reliable anchor. Despite higher borrowing costs and elevated inflation, households continue to benefit from low unemployment and the wealth accumulated from rising equity and real estate prices.

Consumer spending—roughly 70% of U.S. GDP—expanded by nearly 3% year-over-year in the first half of the year, outpacing the contribution from tech investment and reaffirming its role as the primary engine of economic growth.

AI should therefore be viewed not as a replacement for the consumer, but as a complementary growth driver, one that can bolster the expansion, but cannot sustain it alone.

Investor implications

As tech giants continue to invest heavily in AI infrastructure, the theme is expected to play a larger role in shaping the macroeconomic landscape, and raises two key implications:

  1. Favorable macro environment: If AI yields productivity and economic gains, the U.S. could experience a more favorable macroeconomic climate, expanding the stock market rally beyond a select group of dominant stocks to broader beneficiaries of AI adoption.
  2. Vulnerability to corrections: Conversely, AI’s growing influence also raises concerns about potential corrections. If major tech firms reduce AI investments or fail to meet earnings expectations, or firms in other sectors and industries fail to monetize anticipated AI-related productivity growth, that could amplify downside risks in the markets. What has driven economic growth this year could just as easily contribute to future market downturns.

2025 has been shaped by intense enthusiasm for AI and innovation. The coming year, however, will demand a different lens—one focused on balance-sheet strength, earnings quality, and the durability of growth. With valuations elevated and AI-related debt climbing, the risks of overheating are real. But resilient fundamentals provide a counterweight. Ultimately, the next chapter of the AI era will hinge less on excitement and more on execution: which companies can transform investment into lasting value, and which cannot.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Will Santa Fire Up The S&P Sleigh?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Is Santa ready to fire up the S&P 500 Index (SPX) sleigh? That would fit with the historical pattern this time of year - and the Federal Reserve’s rate decision gives us a fundamental catalyst, too.

Check out the MoneyShow Chart of the Day here, which comes courtesy of LPL Financial. In their words: "Historically, the index tends to hover around the flatline during the first half of December, with upward momentum building around the 11th trading day."

(Source: LPL Research)

Guess where we are now? Yep... right at “Punch it Rudolph” time.

It doesn’t hurt that the last major potential obstacle to a stock market advance - a monetary policy disappointment - just got pushed aside. Policymakers didn’t vote unanimously in favor of a Fed cut at this week’s meeting. But they delivered a 25-basis point reduction, and that qualifies as “good enough” for the bulls.

Not every stock is participating in this rally (I’m still looking at you, Oracle Corp. (ORCL)!) Key questions about the lackluster job market, lousy consumer sentiment, relatively sticky inflation, and the K-shaped economy will need answering in 2026.

But in the short term, the stage could be a set for a run into year end. So, keep your eyes out for Santa!

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: Market Fetes Planet Labs' Earnings Beat, But High Multiples, Dilution Risks Loom
data.description:
When last we touched on Planet Labs PBC (PL) in October, the stock had just surged on revenue growth centered around their plan to transition from a data-first company to a satellite maker. I downgraded the stock at the time, from a buy to a hold, because the multiples were starting to look unsustainably high. The price slowly dropped, suggesting momentum was weakening, but then today they issued Q3 earnings, and the price is soaring once again.

It was a modest Q3 beat on both revenue and EPS, but the market acted like this was a real game-changer, with the stock price trending up another 35% in a single day. So naturally, we need to break these new earnings down and figure out if the story has really changed so dramatically or if the momentum is simply getting even further out of hand.

The Q3 Beat: Planet Labs Approaches Break-even Again

For the fiscal year of 2026, Planet Labs has beaten non-GAAP EPS estimates by a few cents, bringing the stock to roughly break-even by that standard. In practice, the GAAP numbers were substantially worse, losing 19¢ per share this quarter, far worse than the 7¢ loss they enjoyed in the same quarter of the prior year. In many regards, this isn’t nearly so exciting an earnings release as the 35% gain in share price would suggest.

Q3 FY2025

Q3 FY2026

(Source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

What we see here is improved revenue, but not much else. Margins are no better, unsurprisingly. Indeed, the conference call noted that the adjusted (non-GAAP) gross margin actually dropped from 64% to 60%.

Revenue growth came almost entirely from the Defense and Intelligence sector, which was up 70% year over year on expanding NATO interest. Civil Government was virtually flat, up just 1%, and the Commercial sector was actually down almost 8%. While this may not bother some investors, I see it as concerning that they’re increasingly dependent on military spending, when priorities on that are likely to change wildly in the future, and NATO might not be content with relying on commercial satellite offerings going forward. It strikes me, to be blunt, as a rather unstable sector, and the fact that it’s up 70% this year suggests to me that it’s a difficult one to project going forward.

While higher revenue allowed Planet Labs to slightly decrease their operating loss, the change in value of their warrants added over $43 million to the net GAAP loss and was a big part of why, from a GAAP perspective, losses got worse this year.

We can see here where this trend continued through the fiscal year so far, despite three consecutive non-GAAP EPS beats.

Q1-Q3 FY2025

(Source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

Planet Labs’ growth is shrinking their losses, which is a bright spot, albeit not that big of one, since the revenue isn’t growing so much that we could expect to reach GAAP break-even consistently with stable margins in the near future. Non-GAAP is necessary to massage the numbers to that point, though the positive free cash flow is an encouraging sign of progress.

That’s not to say that Planet Labs has resolved the multiple problem. If anything, Thursday’s share price surge has compounded the issue and potentially created a new near-term risk – dilution.

Multiples and the New Dilution Problem

To start with, we need to look at the balance sheet. In October, the price/book ratio was a very high 10.49, and unsurprisingly, it’s gotten no better.

Cash and Equivalents

$443 million

Total Current Assets

$754 million

Total Current Liabilities

$189 million

Convertible Notes

$446 million

Total Shareholder Equity

$350 million

(Source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

We see a strong current ratio of almost 4.0 and an extremely high price/book ratio of 15.68, which absolutely dwarfs the sector median's already relatively high 3.18. The current assets are high because of a strong cash position, but we can see that it came at a substantial price for Planet Labs, which issued $460 million in convertible notes in September.

That creates an immediate problem, too. Those notes are convertible, and the initial conversion rate is 83.6715 shares per $1,000 of notes, which gives us a rate of around $11.95 per share. The conversion was only meant to be convertible in 2030, or under some very specific circumstances, but one of those circumstances is if they are 30% over the conversion price for 20 trading days in the last 30 trading days of a quarter.

Today’s momentum surge puts Planet Labs stock substantially over that, and barring a large pullback taking it below the $15.50 per share range, there is every reason to expect the notes will be profitably convertible starting next quarter, after January 31.

This isn’t a small amount of stock we’re talking about either. If the full $460 million in notes were converted at this rate, that amounts to adding about 12% to the number of outstanding Class A shares. That’s a lot of dilution and only makes Planet Labs’ already excessive multiples even higher.

Since Planet Labs isn’t GAAP profitable to begin with, they likely needed to take on some debt to continue a period of rather high CapEx. Doing it as a convertible note, however, may in retrospect have been a poor decision, especially when momentum is driving the stock up to unlikely highs.

Beyond dilution, I think the risk is that government spending priorities shift away from Planet Labs’ offerings. I had once envisioned Planet Labs as a company more heavily focused on the commercial sector, but the numbers suggest that right now, it’s the military and intelligence communities driving growth, and their priorities can change on a moment’s notice. A new focus that doesn’t require Planet Labs’ offerings, and suddenly that aspect of growth, the only really sizable one, could look like less of a route to profitability.

It’s possible that Planet Labs’ track for growth continues to work, and work in such a way that the company becomes a consistently profitable venture. Even then, however, I think the market needs to accept multiples that look vastly higher than what the sector generally commands. Whether that will persist remains to be seen.

Pricing Is Getting Out of Hand for Planet Labs’ Numbers

In the end, as much as I hate to do it, I’m going to downgrade Planet Labs from a hold to a sell, entirely on what I believe is momentum disconnected from reality. I just don’t see Planet Labs where it is being worth anywhere near 15 times their underlying book value, and at the current prices, we have to consider substantial dilution from the convertible notes as an inevitability.

I think there’s a good chance Planet Labs does get to the point where they’ll be consistently break-even, or even slightly profitable, but there’s so much positive news priced into the shares already, I just don’t see a fundamental justification to own these shares at this price. If I owned Planet Labs stock, I’d be looking to take some profits. I’m not even saying momentum can’t continue to drive these prices higher, but I don’t see this rise as sustainable. Not by the numbers, anyhow.

Investors who do decide to stick it out should keep an eye on the prices as they relate to the convertible notes. Unless the stock sheds its recent gains in pretty short order, we’re going to see conversion, dilution, and then the risk that lenders will be dumping these shares on the market to take profits. The market hasn’t seemed to appreciate this risk yet, which is why I suggest reconsidering positions before that happens.
